subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! In this unit you will master one of the most useful tools in all of mathematics: the percentage. Percentages are everywhere in daily life. When Amina sees a market stall in Hargeisa advertising 25% off on fabric, when Yusuf reads that a football striker has a 75% shot-accuracy record, or when Hassan's family works out how much sadaqah to give from the month's savings, they are all thinking in percentages. Learning to handle them confidently will help you make smart decisions with money, understand the news, and read data like an expert.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what a percentage means as a number of parts out of 100.
Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages in both directions.
Recall common equivalents such as 1/2 = 0.5 = 50% without stopping to calculate.
Find a percentage of a quantity using the decimal method, the fraction method and mental strategies.
Work out a percentage increase or decrease and apply it using a multiplier.
Calculate the percentage change between an original value and a new value.
Solve reverse percentage problems to find an original amount.
Handle compound percentage changes applied one after another.
Interpret percentage information in real situations and spot when it might be misleading.

concepts.title: What a Percentage Really Means
concepts.explanation: A percentage is a way of expressing a number as a fraction of 100. The symbol % means “out of 100.” So when you read 25%, you should hear in your head “25 out of every 100,” which is the fraction 25/100. Picture a large square grid divided into 100 tiny squares, like a wall of 100 small tiles in the market. If 25 of those tiles are painted blue, then 25% of the wall is blue. This picture – the hundred grid – is worth keeping in your mind throughout the unit. Why are percentages so useful? Because they give us a…
concepts.example: Percentages are not limited to the range 0 to 100. A percentage can be greater than 100%. If a young mango tree grows from 20 cm to 50 cm, its new height is 250% of its original height, because 50 is two-and-a-half…

concepts.title: Converting Fractions, Decimals and Percentages
concepts.explanation: Fractions, decimals and percentages are three different costumes worn by the same idea – a part of a whole. A confident mathematician can change any one form into the others without hesitation. Let us learn each direction carefully. To turn a fraction into a percentage, multiply by 100%. For example, 1/4 × 100% = 25%, and 3/5 × 100% = 60%, and 7/8 × 100% = 87.5%. A quick way to think about it: find an equivalent fraction with 100 on the bottom, and the top number is your percentage. Since 1/4 is the same as…
concepts.example: To turn a decimal into a percentage, again multiply by 100. So 0.25 becomes 25%, 0.6 becomes 60%, and 0.875 becomes 87.5%. A handy shortcut is that multiplying by 100 moves the decimal point two places to the right. Be…

concepts.title: Finding a Percentage of a Quantity
concepts.explanation: One of the most common tasks is finding a percentage of an amount – for instance, 30% of 80 goats, or 15% of a 40-shilling bill. There are three reliable methods, and it is smart to know all three so you can pick the easiest one for each problem. Change the percentage into a decimal, then multiply. To find 30% of 80, write 30% as 0.30 and calculate 0.30 × 80 = 24. This method is quick with a calculator and works for any numbers.
concepts.example: Change the percentage into a fraction, then multiply. To find 30% of 80, write 30% as 30/100 = 3/10, then 3/10 × 80 = 240/10 = 24. This method shines when the fraction is simple, such as 50% (a half) or 25% (a quarter).

concepts.title: Percentage Increase and Decrease
concepts.explanation: Often we do not just want a percentage of an amount – we want to change an amount by a percentage. Prices rise, populations grow, and savings earn interest. The neat way to do this is with a multiplier, a single decimal you multiply by. To increase a number by a percentage, multiply by (100% + the percentage), written as a decimal. To increase 50 by 20%, the multiplier is 100% + 20% = 120% = 1.20, so the new amount is 50 × 1.20 = 60. The whole original is the 100%, and the extra 20% is added on, giving 120% of the…
concepts.example: To decrease a number by a percentage, multiply by (100% − the percentage). To decrease 80 by 15%, the multiplier is 100% − 15% = 85% = 0.85, so the new amount is 80 × 0.85 = 68. You are keeping 85% of the original and…

concepts.title: Calculating the Percentage Change
concepts.explanation: Sometimes we know the old value and the new value, and we want to know by what percentage it changed. This is the percentage change, and it has one clear formula that you should learn by heart: Percentage change = (new value − original value) ÷ original value × 100%
concepts.example: The result tells you two things at once. If it is positive, the value went up, and the size of the number is the percentage increase. If it is negative, the value went down, and the size of the number is the percentage…

concepts.title: Reverse Percentage Problems
concepts.explanation: A reverse percentage problem gives you the amount after a change and asks for the amount before it. These feel harder at first, but the multiplier makes them straightforward: since multiplying by the multiplier goes forwards, dividing by the multiplier goes backwards. Consider this: after a 20% pay rise, Omar's monthly wage is now 36,000 shillings. What was it before? The rise means the new wage is 120% of the old, so the multiplier is 1.20. To reverse it, divide: 36,000 ÷ 1.20 = 30,000 shillings. We can check by…
concepts.example: Now a decrease example. After a 15% discount, a jacket costs 68 shillings. What was the original price? A 15% discount means the sale price is 85% of the original, so the multiplier is 0.85. Reverse it by dividing: 68 ÷…

methods.title: How to find a percentage of an amount
methods.example: Express 7/20 as a decimal and a percentage.
steps: Change the percentage to a decimal by dividing by 100.
Multiply the decimal by the amount.
Check with a mental estimate built from 10% and 1%.

methods.title: How to work out a percentage change
methods.example: A school has 480 students. 35% are in Year 7. How many Year 7 students are there?
steps: Subtract the original from the new value to get the change.
Divide the change by the original amount.
Multiply by 100% and state whether it is an increase or a decrease.

methods.title: How to solve a reverse percentage problem
methods.example: A salary of 45,000 shillings increases by 8%. What is the new salary?
steps: Decide what percentage of the original the given amount represents.
Write this as a multiplier (for example 120% = 1.20).
Divide the given amount by the multiplier to find the original.
Check by working forwards again.

methods.title: Percentage decrease with a multiplier
methods.example: A car worth 18,000 shillings loses 15% of its value. What is it now worth?
steps: Solution: The multiplier for a 15% decrease is 100% − 15% = 85% = 0.85.
So the value is 18,000 × 0.85 = 15,300 shillings.
Check the percentage decrease with a multiplier result carefully.

methods.title: Calculate a percentage change
methods.example: A stock price rises from 25 to 32. Find the percentage increase.
steps: Solution: Change = 32 − 25 = 7.
Divide by the original: 7 ÷ 25 = 0.28.
Multiply by 100%: 28% increase.
We divided by 25, the original value, not by 32.

methods.title: A reverse percentage problem
methods.example: After a 30% discount, a jacket costs 84 shillings. What was the original price?
steps: Solution: A 30% discount means the sale price is 70% of the original, so the multiplier is 0.70.
Divide to reverse: 84 ÷ 0.70 = 120 shillings.
Check forwards: 120 × 0.70 = 84.
Correct.

workedExamples.title: Convert a fraction to a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: Express 7/20 as a decimal and a percentage.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Make the denominator 100 by multiplying top and bottom by 5: 7/20 = 35/100. So 7/20 = 0.35 = 35%. As a check, 7 ÷ 20 = 0.35, and 0.35 × 100 = 35%.

workedExamples.title: Find a percentage of a quantity
workedExamples.prompt: A school has 480 students. 35% are in Year 7. How many Year 7 students are there?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use the decimal method: 35% = 0.35, so 0.35 × 480 = 168 students. Mental check: 10% of 480 is 48, so 30% is 144; 5% is 24; 144 + 24 = 168. The two methods agree.

workedExamples.title: Percentage increase with a multiplier
workedExamples.prompt: A salary of 45,000 shillings increases by 8%. What is the new salary?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The multiplier for an 8% increase is 100% + 8% = 108% = 1.08. So the new salary is 45,000 × 1.08 = 48,600 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Percentage decrease with a multiplier
workedExamples.prompt: A car worth 18,000 shillings loses 15% of its value. What is it now worth?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The multiplier for a 15% decrease is 100% − 15% = 85% = 0.85. So the value is 18,000 × 0.85 = 15,300 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Calculate a percentage change
workedExamples.prompt: A stock price rises from 25 to 32. Find the percentage increase.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change = 32 − 25 = 7. Divide by the original: 7 ÷ 25 = 0.28. Multiply by 100%: 28% increase. We divided by 25, the original value, not by 32.

workedExamples.title: A reverse percentage problem
workedExamples.prompt: After a 30% discount, a jacket costs 84 shillings. What was the original price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A 30% discount means the sale price is 70% of the original, so the multiplier is 0.70. Divide to reverse: 84 ÷ 0.70 = 120 shillings. Check forwards: 120 × 0.70 = 84. Correct.

workedExamples.title: A compound change
workedExamples.prompt: A value increases by 20%, then decreases by 10%. What is the overall percentage change?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Combine the multipliers: 1.20 × 0.90 = 1.08. That is a multiplier of 1.08, which means an 8% increase overall. Adding 20% and −10% to get 10% would be wrong, because the second change acts on a larger amount.

workedExamples.title: A reverse compound problem
workedExamples.prompt: A computer's price drops 20% in one month, then a further 15% the next month. The final price is 680 shillings. What was the original price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The combined multiplier is 0.80 × 0.85 = 0.68. To reverse, divide: 680 ÷ 0.68 = 1,000 shillings. Check forwards: 1,000 × 0.80 = 800, then 800 × 0.85 = 680. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Find the percentage change
workedExamples.prompt: Find the percentage change: a) 40 to 50 b) 80 to 64 c) 25 to 32.
workedExamples.solution: a) 25% increase, b) 20% decrease, c) 28% increase.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Maria scored 85% on a test with 40 questions. How many did she answer correctly?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A recipe needs 2.5 cups of flour. How much flour is needed to make 80% of the recipe?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: After a 25% increase, a value becomes 150. Find the original value.

activities.title: The Hundred Grid
steps: Draw a 10 by 10 grid, giving 100 small squares.
Shade 25 squares in one colour and 40 squares in another.
Write the percentage each colour represents, and the fraction and decimal too.
Ask a family member to name a percentage and shade exactly that many squares.
The hundred grid makes it easy to *see* that a percentage is simply a number of parts out of 100.

activities.title: Market Discount Hunt
activities.materials: real or pretend prices (write price tags for household items).
steps: Give each item a price in shillings.
Choose a discount for each: 10%, 25% or 50%.
Work out the saving and the sale price using a multiplier.
Compare two offers on the same item and decide which is the better deal.
This trains you to find discounts quickly – a skill you will use in every market for the rest of your life.

activities.title: The Percentage Card Game
activities.materials: cards showing fractions, decimals and percentages that match (for example 1/2, 0.5, 50%).
steps: Make sets of three matching cards (fraction, decimal, percentage).
Shuffle all the cards and deal them out.
Take turns laying down cards and claiming a matching set of three.
The player with the most complete sets wins.
Playing this a few times will make the common equivalents automatic.

activities.title: The Savings Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a calculator if you have one.
steps: Imagine saving 1,000 shillings that grows by 5% each year.
Work out the amount after 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 years using the multiplier 1.05.
Record the results in a table and describe how the growth changes.
Investigate: how many years until the savings first pass 1,300 shillings?
This investigation shows the surprising power of compound growth over time.

activities.title: Percentages in the News (Family Task)
activities.materials: a newspaper, radio report, or a story an adult can share.
steps: Find a statement that uses a percentage, such as a price rise or a survey result.
Discuss with your family what the percentage really means.
Check whether the statement could be misleading – what is the total it is based on?
Write one sentence explaining the percentage clearly in your own words.
This helps you become a careful reader of the percentages that fill the news every day.

activities.title: Fair Shares and Sadaqah
activities.materials: a small amount of pretend money in shillings (paper coins).
steps: Imagine a family saves 8,000 shillings in a month.
Work out 10% of it to give as sadaqah (charity), which can be any amount the giver chooses.
Work out how much remains after giving.
Try again with a different saving amount and a different percentage.
This links percentages to sharing and generosity, showing how the maths supports thoughtful decisions.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: To turn a decimal into a percentage, again multiply by 100. So 0.25 becomes 25%, 0.6 becomes 60%, and 0.875 becomes 87.5%. A handy shortcut is that multiplying by 100 moves the decimal point two places to the right. Be careful with this shortcut, though: 0.6 is 60%, not 6%. Whenever you are unsure, go back to the rule “multiply by 100” and you will not slip.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: The result tells you two things at once. If it is positive, the value went up, and the size of the number is the percentage increase. If it is negative, the value went down, and the size of the number is the percentage decrease. The single most important rule is that you always divide by the original value – the amount you started from – never the new one.

terms: A number of parts out of 100
The decimal you multiply by to apply a change
Growth or fall as a percentage of the original
Finding the original amount before a change
A change applied on top of a previous change
Different forms with the same value (1/4 = 0.25 = 25%)
commonMistakes: Always divide the change by the original
Adding percentages in a compound change
Divide by the multiplier to reverse

realProblems.prompt: At an Eastleigh market, a jacket is priced at 150 shillings. Sahra has a 20% off voucher. a) What is the discounted price? b) If sales tax of 6.5% is then added, what does she pay? c) If she had waited for a 35% sale instead, how much more would she have saved than with the voucher?
realProblems.answer: 1) a) 150 × 0.80 = 120; b) 120 × 1.065 = 127.80; c) the voucher saves 150 − 120 = 30, while a 35% sale gives 150 × 0.65 = 97.50 and saves 150 − 97.50 = 52.50, so the sale saves 22.50 more.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) a) 150 × 0.80 = 120; b) 120 × 1.065 = 127.80; c) the voucher saves 150 − 120 = 30, while a 35% sale gives 150 × 0.65 = 97.50 and saves 150 − 97.50 = 52.50, so the sale saves 22.50 more.

realProblems.prompt: A car dealership near Nairobi: original price 25,000 shillings, dealer markup 20%, then a customer discount of 15%, then sales tax of 8%. Calculate the final price the customer pays.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A town's population changes over three years from a baseline of 15,000: Year 2 increases by 8%, Year 3 decreases by 5%, Year 4 increases by 12%. Find the population at the end of each year and the overall percentage change from Year 1 to Year 4.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Idris invests 5,000 shillings at 3% simple interest per year, while Liban invests 4,500 shillings at 4% per year. How much interest does each earn in one year, and who earns more?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A stock loses 25% of its value, then gains 30%. If it is now worth 97.50 shillings, what was its original value?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: After a 25% increase, a value becomes 150. Find the original value.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

selfAssessment: I can explain what a percentage means as a number of parts out of 100.
I can convert between fractions, decimals and percentages in both directions.
I can recall common equivalents such as 1/2 = 0.5 = 50% without stopping to calculate.
I can find a percentage of a quantity using the decimal method, the fraction method and mental strategies.
I can work out a percentage increase or decrease and apply it using a multiplier.
I can calculate the percentage change between an original value and a new value.
I can solve reverse percentage problems to find an original amount.
I can handle compound percentage changes applied one after another.
